pathophysiology_summary: This patient has known gallstones with a history of self-limiting biliary colic that typically resolves within 1-2 hours. The current episode is qualitatively different: continuous right upper quadrant pain persisting beyond 6 hours, radiating to the right shoulder blade, worsened by deep inspiration, accompanied by fever/chills and vomiting. This pattern indicates that a gallstone has caused persistent cystic duct obstruction leading to gallbladder wall inflammation and secondary infection (acute cholecystitis), rather than the transient obstruction of simple biliary colic. Continuous pain beyond 6 hours with systemic features (fever) is the key distinguishing feature and signals a risk of progression to gallbladder gangrene, perforation, and peritonitis if not treated with IV antibiotics and likely surgery.

differential.evidence_basis: Continuous RUQ pain beyond 6 hours with fever and vomiting in a patient with known gallstones.
Given her known gallstone history, this would be the default assumption, but the unusually long duration and fever argue against it.
Vomiting and feeling unwell could suggest gastroenteritis, but the localised RUQ pain and known gallstone history make this less likely as the primary diagnosis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Duration beyond 6 hours plus fever should distinguish this from simple biliary colic from the outset.

differential.evidence_basis: Pain worsened by deep breathing and radiating to the shoulder blade (DI-004), combined with fever and prolonged duration, strongly supports cholecystitis.
Fever and pain lasting well beyond the typical 1-2 hour resolution window exclude simple colic.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage cholecystitis should be clearly leading, warranting urgent ED referral rather than reassurance.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis requiring urgent in-person assessment, imaging, IV antibiotics, and likely surgical referral.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A plan without same-day in-person/ED referral and NBM advice is inadequate given the fever and prolonged pain.

red_flags.description: Continuous RUQ pain persisting beyond 6 hours, unlike prior self-limiting attacks
red_flags.clinical_significance: Key distinguishing feature between biliary colic and acute cholecystitis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed treatment risks gallbladder gangrene and perforation.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates active gallbladder infection/inflammation rather than simple obstruction.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to sepsis or gallbladder rupture.

red_flags.description: Positive Murphy's sign, mild scleral icterus, and localized RUQ guarding on examination (clinician-only findings)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Objectively supports cholecystitis and raises the possibility of choledocholithiasis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not directly available to the AI Doctor via chat.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on 'known gallstones, same as before'
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: AI Doctor may assume this is just another episode of the patient's usual biliary colic and suggest simple analgesia and a routine GP follow-up, missing that the prolonged duration and fever indicate progression to acute cholecystitis requiring urgent hospital assessment.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day Emergency Department presentation for bloods, ultrasound, IV antibiotics, and surgical review; nil by mouth in the meantime
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Assuming this is simple biliary colic and recommending only oral analgesia risks progression to gallbladder perforation and peritonitis.
